title: Fix CTkEntry place_holder
body:
- Fixed place holder behavior when assigning a textvariable to a CTkEntry
- Added flag to control textvariable_callback event

step_plan.summary: Fixes `CTkEntry` placeholder behavior when a `textvariable` is used by allowing placeholder activation for empty variable states without corrupting the variable, and adds a new flag to enable/disable reacting to `textvariable` write-trace callbacks. Also improves textvariable trace lifecycle management in init/configure and ensures traces are removed on destroy.

steps.operation: Introduce and store a new configuration flag controlling whether the textvariable trace callback should react to writes; ensure initial placeholder state is derived from the initial StringVar value and placeholder settings without causing recursion.
steps.reason: `CTkEntry` currently wires the textvariable trace in `__init__` but has no way to suppress callback behavior; additionally, placeholder initialization must work correctly when a `textvariable` is present at construction.
steps.side_effects: Changes constructor/config API (new parameter); existing code creating `CTkEntry(textvariable=...)` may see different placeholder behavior (now shows placeholder when variable empty). Requires aligning defaults to preserve backwards behavior where appropriate.

steps.operation: Adjust placeholder activation logic to work even when a textvariable is assigned (e.g., allow placeholder when the variable/entry content is empty) while avoiding writing placeholder text into the StringVar (preventing recursion/state corruption).
steps.reason: Current implementation blocks placeholder activation when `self._textvariable` is set, which breaks expected placeholder behavior for `StringVar`-backed entries. The fix must ensure placeholder is a visual state and does not fight the underlying variable content.
steps.side_effects: May alter how `get()` behaves under placeholder state when textvariable is used; must ensure no infinite trace loops and no unintended `StringVar.set()` triggered by placeholder insertion.

steps.operation: Update the textvariable write-trace handler to honor the new enable/disable flag and to keep placeholder state consistent for both transitions: empty -> show placeholder (if appropriate) and non-empty -> deactivate placeholder.
steps.reason: The callback currently only activates placeholder on empty and does not support suppression. It should early-return when disabled and also deactivate placeholder when external code sets a non-empty value, ensuring UI reflects the variable accurately.
steps.side_effects: Programmatic `StringVar.set()` will now potentially deactivate placeholder immediately; disabling the flag will stop placeholder auto-management from variable updates, which may affect apps relying on current callback side effects.

steps.operation: Enhance configure-time textvariable handling: when `configure(textvariable=...)` replaces/assigns a variable, properly remove any existing trace, attach a new trace (depending on the flag), store trace handle/id, and re-evaluate placeholder state; also allow configuring the new callback-enabled flag post-init.
steps.reason: `configure(textvariable=...)` currently doesn’t manage trace lifecycle, causing missing behavior (no callback) or potential duplicates if added elsewhere. It must be consistent with `__init__`, support runtime updates, and integrate the new flag.
steps.side_effects: Runtime reconfiguration may change behavior for apps that swap variables; improper trace management can cause duplicate callbacks—this step must ensure trace removal works across tkinter versions. Updating the flag at runtime may require re-binding traces or toggling callback behavior.

steps.operation: On widget destruction, cleanly detach the textvariable trace callback (if attached) before delegating to base destroy logic.
steps.reason: Without trace removal, `StringVar` can retain callbacks to destroyed widgets, leading to leaks and errors; with improved trace support in `configure`, cleanup becomes essential.
steps.side_effects: If external code expects the trace to remain (unlikely), behavior changes; ensures fewer post-destroy exceptions when variables are updated later. Must be compatible with both `trace_add/trace_remove` and any fallback trace APIs used in the project.
